title: A list of pairs
checkLabels.t1: There is a list of pairs
why.no-dl: It is still an ordinary list. The specs are not four things — they are four PAIRS. There is a tag for exactly that: <dl>.
why.no-dt: There is a wrapper, but no names. <dt> is the name — "Frame", "Wheels".
why.no-dd: There are names, but no values. <dd> is the answer — "Aluminium", "26 inches".
why.still-ul: The old <ul> is still there. Replace it — you have something more precise now.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
blocks.0.text: You are adding the bike's specifications. Every line is a pair: a name and a value. Frame — aluminium. Wheels — 26 inches.
blocks.1.text: You did it with a list and a colon. It works. But look at what your code says: "here are four things of one kind". They are not four things. They are four PAIRS. The colon is something only you know about.
blocks.2.text: There is a tag for exactly this
blocks.3.text: <dl> — a description list. Inside: <dt> is the name (the term), <dd> is the value (the description).
blocks.7.text: Specifications. A glossary. Questions and answers. Metadata — author, date, category. Anywhere there is a name and an answer.
blocks.8.text: One name can have several values — you just put two <dd> in a row. And the other way round: two names sharing one value.
blocks.9.text: Notice the browser indents the values by itself. Again, you never told it to.
blocks.11.text: Google reads <dl> and understands this is a spec — it can show it straight in the results. From a <ul> with colons it understands nothing.
blocks.12.text: The same on screen. Different for everything else.
